title: Personal Data Protection Code of Practice (Communications) 2022
summary: This sector-specific Code applies to all licensees in the Communications class (Class 1 of the Class of Data Users Order 2013), including telecommunications companies, internet service providers, mobile network operators, and content application service providers. It supplements the General Code 2022 with enhanced requirements reflecting the uniquely large volumes and sensitivity of communications data, including call records, location data, and internet usage data. The Code takes into account requirements under the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 and MCMC directions.

provisions.title: Scope and Application
provisions.content: This Code applies to all Class 1 licensees under the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998, including network facilities providers, network service providers, applications service providers, and content applications service providers. Special attention is given to the protection of communications metadata (call detail records, location data, internet usage records), which is inherently sensitive as it reveals patterns of personal communication and daily life.
requirements: All licensees must map their personal data processing activities including metadata collection
Communications data must be classified by sensitivity level in the ROPA
This Code must be read together with the General Code 2022 and applicable MCMC directions

provisions.title: Subscriber Registration Data
provisions.content: Personal data collected in the course of subscriber registration (including the mandatory SIM registration process) must be: (a) used only for the purposes of providing telecommunications services, regulatory compliance, and lawful interception; (b) not used for marketing purposes without separate consent; (c) verified for accuracy at the point of registration; (d) retained in accordance with MCMC and PDPA requirements; and (e) secured with the highest level of security given that SIM registration data includes identity card numbers and biometric data. SIM registration data must not be sold or disclosed to third parties except for regulatory purposes or fraud prevention.
requirements: SIM registration data is sensitive — treat as high-risk personal data
SIM registration data must not be used for commercial profiling without consent
SIM registration retention: duration of subscription plus 2 years minimum
Unauthorized disclosure of SIM registration data is a serious offence under both CMA and PDPA

provisions.title: Call Detail Records and Communications Metadata
provisions.content: Call detail records (CDRs), SMS metadata, and internet session records collected in the course of network operations must: (a) be used only for billing, network management, fraud prevention, and lawful interception compliance; (b) not be used for profiling or behavioral targeting without explicit subscriber consent; (c) be encrypted at rest and in transit; (d) be accessible only to network operations, billing, and lawful interception compliance teams; (e) be retained for no longer than 12 months for billing purposes unless a longer period is required by MCMC direction or lawful interception obligations; and (f) be provided to law enforcement only pursuant to lawful production orders or court orders.
requirements: CDRs must not be used for commercial profiling without consent
12-month retention maximum for billing purposes
Lawful interception must be documented and handled by a designated compliance team
Access to CDR systems must be strictly controlled and logged

provisions.title: Location Data
provisions.content: Location data (cell tower location, GPS location from applications, Wi-Fi positioning) is highly sensitive personal data that reveals the physical movements and patterns of life of subscribers. Communications data controllers processing location data must: (a) provide specific and prominent disclosure of location data collection in the subscriber privacy notice; (b) obtain explicit consent for location data processing beyond that necessary for network operation and emergency services; (c) implement real-time and retrospective location data access controls; (d) not retain granular location history beyond 3 months without explicit consent; and (e) not share location data with third parties (including advertising networks) without specific consent for each category of sharing.
requirements: Location data consent must be specific to the purpose (network operation vs. third-party sharing)
Granular location history retention: maximum 3 months without specific consent
Real-time location sharing with third parties requires explicit consent
Location data requests from law enforcement require lawful authority

provisions.title: Security of Network Data Systems
provisions.content: Communications data controllers must implement security measures appropriate to the significant volumes and sensitivity of subscriber data, including: (a) network security architecture isolating subscriber data systems from internet-facing systems; (b) privileged access management for network administration accounts; (c) end-to-end encryption for subscriber data at rest and in transit; (d) comprehensive security information and event management (SIEM) capabilities for real-time monitoring of subscriber data system access; (e) regular penetration testing (at least twice annually) for systems holding subscriber data; and (f) cyber threat intelligence sharing with MCMC and CyberSecurity Malaysia.
requirements: Subscriber data systems must be on segregated network segments
Privileged access requires MFA and is subject to additional logging
Penetration testing twice yearly minimum
Threat intelligence sharing with MCMC is encouraged and supports sector-wide security

provisions.title: Direct Marketing by Communications Providers
provisions.content: Communications service providers engaging in direct marketing to subscribers must: (a) clearly distinguish between service communications (billing, service changes) and marketing communications; (b) obtain separate, specific consent for marketing communications including in-app notifications, SMS marketing, and email marketing; (c) honour opt-out requests within 7 days and cease all marketing channels unless the subscriber has provided separate consent for each; (d) comply with the Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024 requirements on marketing consent; and (e) not condition service provision on consent to marketing — subscribers must be able to reject marketing while retaining their service.
requirements: Separate consent required for marketing — service consent is not sufficient
Opt-out must be honoured within 7 days for all marketing channels
Marketing must not be conditioned on service provision
Suppression lists must be maintained and applied to all marketing campaigns
